This is very interesting, thanks for sharing this link! The way I understand this is, that the LSB needs to be considered nibble wise? You should get this to work with an external decoding. You can use this python one for example:
```python
#!/usr/bin/env python

import sys

bits = sys.argv[1]

# padding
while len(bits) % 8 != 0:
    bits += "0"

result = []
for i in range(0, len(bits), 8):
    result.append("".join(reversed(bits[i+4:i+8])))
    result.append("".join(reversed(bits[i:i+4])))

print("".join(result))
```
Just save the code to a file and specify the filename when creating a new decoding with ``` External Program ``` in decoding dialog.

Let me know if this helps!

What is this image of?? ;)

![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/970135/23521946/689f9ae6-ff3e-11e6-8b1a-04a3e9c7e38e.png)

### Zoom in:

![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/970135/23522186/46933ee8-ff3f-11e6-9c6b-ec651870fbeb.png)


I think the detection threshold is too low. `bkg.globalrms` is about 3.5, so the threshold is about 10.5, which is very small compared to the features in the image. Therefore, you're getting one (or a few) giant objects, and then the deblending algorithm is trying to separate out all the little blobs. Setting a higher initial threshold should help.

SEP / SExtractor *can* still work on this image, but the defaults (and assumptions in the code) are set for wide-field optical/IR imaging. Those defaults and assumptions don't make sense for this image, so you'll need to be more careful and tweak a few things. For example, the background estimation algorithm in SEP / SExtractor basically assumes that the majority of pixels in any region are background. That doesn't seem to be the case here, so you may need a custom background estimation (or to be careful about what you define as "background" anyway).

Looking at the code, this happens when the background annulus has zero area (when using the `bkgann` option). This would indeed happen when the source is out of the image.

By default, cython makes division work like in pure Python, where `1.0 / 0.0` raises an error. I could change it to make it (1) emit a warning instead of an error, as in numpy (`np.array(1.) / np.array(0.)`) or (2) no warning or error.

I think maybe (1) would be better. People are used to this behavior from numpy.

 I think the return value would be `-inf` in either case. Returning `nan` would be nicer, but this would require more work - I'd want to ensure that apertures *not* using `bkgann` also return nan when entirely off the image. I don't think that happens now.

For huge `reads.fasta`, the general strategy is to split it into smaller files and then map each part against the whole file on each node. Something like:
```sh
split -l 1000000 reads.fasta
minimap2 -t8 -x ava-pb reads.fasta xaa > xaa.paf  # on node 1
minimap2 -t8 -x ava-pb reads.fasta xab > xab.paf  # on node 2
...
```

Your Mopidy config file (ie `~/.config/mopidy/mopidy.conf`) will need to allow connections from all IP addresses. For example:

```
[mpd]
hostname = 0.0.0.0
[http]
hostname = 0.0.0.0
```

Then restart Mopidy server. From your phone, navigate to http://150.201.183.214:6680. This IP address looks like a remote IP address (not a local network) so you'll need to have port forwarding configured on your router. I would recommend ensuring your phone is on the same network as your Mopidy server.

For more reading: https://docs.mopidy.com/en/latest/ext/mpd/#confval-mpd/hostname

As a matter of follow-up, the issue appears to be that where we were previously re-constructing the `attributes` of a `.response`, our adapting to ldap3 2.x meant we were using `response['attributes']` directly, but that object is a `CaseInsensitiveDict` which isn't JSON-serializable, so we'll need to wrap attributes before serializing them. I mention that here because I don't feel it represents an issue with ldap3, but just our usage of it.

Replacing auto_ptr with unique_ptr is possible only if auto_ptr is not copied in the original code. The difference between auto_ptr and the unique_ptr is that the latter only has move semantics when used as a source of assignment, that what makes it unque. If it was never used in a code with copy semantics, I think it's safe to blindly replace it.

The other important thing if std::auto_ptr was reintroduced when rolling back my patches, then it's fully legitimate reason for the compiler to refuse compiling such code under C++17. The standard mandates such behaviour, and the only thing to fix whatever is upsetting VC2017 is to fix the standard. This is a rather new thing in C++ world, when a standard mandates removal of dangerous features.

@austinhartzheim thank you very much for this very thorough analysis of the problem at hand.

I have a few things to add:

- I think we should not be shy to just try a request and see if it fails or not. I think this is a very pythonic way of handling things anyway. So I wouldn't bother to check and measure the optimal size, but rather use a good default, try the request, and if it fails, maybe try to split it differently, and if it still fails, raise the error to the user
- I think the places you tracked down are very good the come up with a good default value and/or influence the definition of an actual limit in the API as you already mentioned. But it still leaves us with at least two problems:
    1. People might still use an old version of the API
    2. People might run `osmapi` against their very own custom version of the API, running on their server or customized code, not running on Apache

All these points lead me to the conclusion, that I'd prefer a *limit with a good default value*, that a user of `osmapi` can override (e.g. in the constructor). If the limit is reached, another request is sent to the OSM API with the remaining items, the results are then put back together and returned to the consumer as "one", so that this whole process is transparent from a users perspective (i.e. they don't notice it).

Hi all, and  sorry for not being involved in the discussion. I'm very interested in this discussion, but I'm also busy with other projects at the moment :( 

Anyway, this issue touches two problems that are the main sources of complexity in gpxpy. One is that gpxpy can work both with the minidom and lxml parsers (and that means you don't use the "normal" xml parser and dom manipulation, but my own xml "abstraction"). The other problem is that gpxpy uses the same object model for both gpx 1.0 and gpx 1.1. 

Regarding the latter, when I was rewriting gpxpy for 1.1, I wanted to create a way to "describe" the xml<->gpxpy_models transformations, instead of coding it for all fields. I ended up with something like this:

    class GPXTrack:
        gpx_10_fields = [
                mod_gpxfield.GPXField('name'),
                mod_gpxfield.GPXField('comment', 'cmt'),
                mod_gpxfield.GPXField('description', 'desc'),
                mod_gpxfield.GPXField('source', 'src'),
                mod_gpxfield.GPXField('link', 'url'),
                mod_gpxfield.GPXField('link_text', 'urlname'),
                mod_gpxfield.GPXField('number', type=mod_gpxfield.INT_TYPE),
                mod_gpxfield.GPXComplexField('segments', tag='trkseg', classs=GPXTrackSegment, is_list=True),
        ]
        gpx_11_fields = [
                mod_gpxfield.GPXField('name'),
                mod_gpxfield.GPXField('comment', 'cmt'),
                mod_gpxfield.GPXField('description', 'desc'),
                mod_gpxfield.GPXField('source', 'src'),
                'link',
                    mod_gpxfield.GPXField('link', attribute='href'),
                    mod_gpxfield.GPXField('link_text', tag='text'),
                    mod_gpxfield.GPXField('link_type', tag='type'),
                '/link',
                mod_gpxfield.GPXField('number', type=mod_gpxfield.INT_TYPE),
                mod_gpxfield.GPXField('type'),
                mod_gpxfield.GPXExtensionsField('extensions'),
                mod_gpxfield.GPXComplexField('segments', tag='trkseg', classs=GPXTrackSegment, is_list=True),
        ]

https://github.com/tkrajina/gpxpy/blob/master/gpxpy/gpx.py#L1307

Basically, the `gpx_10_fields` array describes how to marshal and unmarshal the xml from/to the object for gpx 1.0. And of course `gpx_11_fields` is the same, but for gpx 1.1. 

Since gpx extension can contain complex xml subtrees, I was thinking that we can use the same "descriptors" to marshal/unmarshal those extension XML subtrees into custom objects. And those objects would use the same descriptors.

For example, a simple GPX extension could be defined like:

    class ExampleGPXExtension:
        __desc__ = [
                mod_gpxfield.GPXField('name'),
                mod_gpxfield.GPXField('comment', 'cmt'),
                mod_gpxfield.GPXField('description', 'desc'),
                mod_gpxfield.GPXField('source', 'src'),
                'link',
                    mod_gpxfield.GPXField('link', attribute='href'),
                '/link',
        ]
        func __init__(self):
            self.name = None
            self.comment = None
            self.description = None
            self.source = None
            self.link = None

Ideas/criticism(s) are welcome :)

Surely I would love to help, but this is not as simple as I thought it would be. I avoided the point that the method has to return immutable object, that is why the whole dictionary is reconstructed.

But when I noticed again, I found that the method itself has a bug. Since it just copies the value part from the original object, if anything changes in the original `obj` will also change in the newly constructed `obj` too, making this method only half-immutable.

Example:
```python
from pydash import py_

obj = {'a': {'b': 1, 'c': 2}, 'd': 1}
n_obj = py_.rename_keys(obj, {'a': 'A', 'd': 'D'})
obj['a']['b'] = 2
obj['a']['b'] == n_obj['A']['b'] # will return True which is probably wrong if not expected behaviour
```
So there will be different approaches considering whether the method has to return mutable or immutable object. If immutable we will have to first do deep copy over the object and then we can simply use `py_.get`, `py_.set` and `py_.unset` to rename the keys.

The best way to install the NLTK data on Heroku is to use the built-in `post_compile` hook in the Python buildpack.  By using this approach, you won't have to add the data to your git repo.

https://github.com/heroku/heroku-buildpack-python/blob/master/bin/steps/hooks/post_compile

Here's how to do it:

1. Create a `bin` directory in root of your project
2. Inside the `bin` directory, create `post_compile` and `install_textblob_corpora` text files using the contents of this gist: https://gist.github.com/godshall/a27821c0d1030a096c12 (also added to the bottom of this message)
3. Commit these files to your git repo
4. Set the `NLTK_DATA` environment variable to `/app/nltk_data`.  `$ heroku config:set NLTK_DATA='/app/nltk_data'`
5. Deploy to Heroku

That's it!  

bin/post_compile

    #!/usr/bin/env bash

    if [ -f bin/install_textblob_corpora ]; then
        echo "-----> Running install_textblob_corpora"
        chmod +x bin/install_textblob_corpora
        bin/install_textblob_corpora
    fi

    echo "-----> Post-compile done"

bin/install_textblob_corpora

    #!/usr/bin/env bash

    source $BIN_DIR/utils

    echo "-----> Starting corpora installation"

    # Assumes NLTK_DATA environment variable is already set
    # $ heroku config:set NLTK_DATA='/app/nltk_data'

    # Install the default corpora to NLTK_DATA directory
    python -m textblob.download_corpora

    # Open the NLTK_DATA directory
    cd ${NLTK_DATA}

    # Delete all of the zip files in the NLTK DATA directory
    find . -name "*.zip" -type f -delete

    echo "-----> Finished corpora installation"

In the read case, the fields are generated as flat ``string``s because ``drf-yasg`` has no idea about how to handle your ``ChoiceFieldSerializer`` field class. The write case is caused by the hardcoding I talked about above. It could be solved inside ``drf-yasg`` by peeking inside the underlying model field to inspect its type, but it could prove a little too presumptious.

For both cases I think you could solve it with a [custom ``FieldInspector``](https://drf-yasg.readthedocs.io/en/stable/custom_spec.html#inspector-classes) added to [DEFAULT_FIELD_INSPECTORS](https://drf-yasg.readthedocs.io/en/stable/settings.html#default-field-inspectors). See also [FieldInspector](https://drf-yasg.readthedocs.io/en/stable/drf_yasg.html#drf_yasg.inspectors.FieldInspector).

Probably something along the lines of (kinda-pseudo-code, meaning I didn't try to run it):

Read case:
```python
class CustomChoiceFieldInspector(FieldInspector):
    def field_to_swagger_object(self, field, swagger_object_type, use_references, **kwargs):
        # this returns a callable which extracts title, description and other stuff for you
        # https://drf-yasg.readthedocs.io/en/stable/_modules/drf_yasg/inspectors/base.html#FieldInspector._get_partial_types
        SwaggerType, _ = self._get_partial_types(field, swagger_object_type, use_references, **kwargs)

        if isinstance(field, ChoiceFieldSerializer):  # <---- your custom choices field
             integer_choice_field = SwaggerType(type=openapi.TYPE_OBJECT, required=["label","value"], properties={"label": openapi.Schema(type=openapi.TYPE_STRING, read_only=True), "value": openapi.Schema(type=openapi.TYPE_INTEGER, read_only=True)})

             # if you want to make it a separate reference
             # if not, "return integer_choice_field" would do
             if use_references:
                 # self.components is a ReferenceResolver: https://drf-yasg.readthedocs.io/en/stable/drf_yasg.html#drf_yasg.openapi.ReferenceResolver
                 definitions = self.components.with_scope(openapi.SCHEMA_DEFINITIONS)
                 definitions.setdefault("IntegerChoiceField", lambda: integer_choice_field)
                 return openapi.SchemaRef(definitions, "IntegerChoiceField)
             else:
                 return integer_choice_field
        

        return NotHandled
```

The write case is more complicated, you would need a way to discern your ``ChoiceField``s from regular ``ChoiceField``s, perhaps by switching on ``field.parent`` and ``field.name``?

```
from pyexcelerate import Workbook, Color, Style, Font, Fill, Format
wb = Workbook()

wb.save("output.xlsx")
```

Hi Steve,

The default Powerpoint template liberally uses object placeholders (as opposed to table / picture / etc placeholder). Maybe I missed the functionality of some other class, but I couldn't find an obvious means of populating an object placeholder with a picture (having heavily used the Powerpoint COM interface, this is a common usage pattern).

By default python-pptx assigns an object of SlidePlaceholder type to placeholders it doesn't currently support. However, this SlidePlaceholder doesn't seem to have any functionality to replace itself with a picture / table / etc. 

Cheers,
James

Update:
Tried gdb, no luck. Tried lldb, no luck.  Tried lldb-3.8, money.

Determined that the issue has to do with [optarg](http://linux.die.net/man/3/optarg)

Not garbage trace:
```
* thread #1: tid = 5025, 0x00007f3b2fbe0aea libc.so.6`__GI_strlen + 42 at strlen.S:106, name = 'uwsgi', stop reason = signal SIGSEGV: invalid address (fault address: 0x0)
  * frame #0: 0x00007f3b2fbe0aea libc.so.6`__GI_strlen + 42 at strlen.S:106
    frame #1: 0x00007f3b2fbe082e libc.so.6`__GI___strdup(s=0x0000000000000000) + 14 at strdup.c:41
    frame #2: 0x00007f3b2a3ebd84 libxt_tcp.so`parse_tcp_ports(portstring=0x0000000000000000, ports=0x00000000015e5cd4) + 29 at libxt_tcp.c:46
    frame #3: 0x00007f3b2a3ec191 libxt_tcp.so`tcp_parse(c=50, argv=0x00007f3b2a61ad00, invert=0, flags=0x00007f3b2a61af40, entry=0x00000000014f84f0, match=0x00007f3b2a61aac0) + 289 at libxt_tcp.c:161
    frame #4: 0x00007f3b2c859c46 libxtwrapper.so`wrap_parse(fn=(libxt_tcp.so`tcp_parse at libxt_tcp.c:143), i=50, argv=0x00007f3b2a61ad00, inv=0, flags=0x00007f3b2a61af40, p=0x00000000014f84f0, mptr=0x00007f3b2a61aac0) + 86 at wrapper.c:39
```


The issue is that `optarg` goes from valid -> null after this line (not sure why, I assume that it is not playing nice with uwsgi): <https://github.com/qris/iptables/blob/d225d979534c2accc864504475e927c6b265f9b3/extensions/libxt_tcp.c#L161>.  Assuming that this is not nooby cli program design, my best bet is that it is a result of how iptables handled argument parsing long ago.

Normally one would expect there to be a single option parse somewhere near the programs entry, like [how uwsgi does it](https://github.com/unbit/uwsgi/blob/3dc786f2c8843151612aebdfc479eb41ee7a004f/core/init.c#L256).


To move past the segfault, you can change this:
``` diff
-    parse_tcp_ports(optarg, tcpinfo->dpts);
+    parse_tcp_ports(argv[1], tcpinfo->dpts);
```
However, there seems to be something else afoot here which then causes `XTablesError: DNAT: parameter error -2`.  Still looking in to a full solution.

Actually, I did not used `StreamReaderProtocol` because :
* IMAP protocol is complex and the end of a command is terminated by a tagged line with OK/KO/BAD
* IMAP RFC tells that the server could send you data at any-time (so you need to implement a main loop with read/write buffer)
* there is two modes of reading data : until the next CRLF or using the litteral length of a FETCH (so you have to implement a minimal state machine to know what to do)
* as it is monothreaded, it is not that hard to buffer the previous incomplete line
* the change seemed easier keeping Protocol as a base class

>But in any case I can put very long url in the xlsx file by hand. And I don't have any problems with this. Why I can't do it in my program?

It isn't possible to create a url (without anchors) longer than 255 characters in Excel. If you enter a longer url then Excel converts it to a string (with blue underline) but it is no longer a link. You can verify this yourself in Excel.

Well, I've just looked at Django source and it has a method called [`BaseHandler.make_view_atomic()`](https://github.com/django/django/blob/0ed7d155635da9f79d4dd67e4889087d3673c6da/django/core/handlers/base.py#L76-L82). It simply applies `transaction.atomic()` (or not) for all database connections based on the value of `ATOMIC_REQUESTS` and a view property called `_non_atomic_requests`. The method is self-contained (does not even use `self`) but it's undocumented.

It [gets called on a view by `BaseHandler.get_response()`](https://github.com/django/django/blob/0ed7d155635da9f79d4dd67e4889087d3673c6da/django/core/handlers/base.py#L130) just before it executes the view itself.

So I'd create a `BaseHandler` instance ([the constructor](https://github.com/django/django/blob/0ed7d155635da9f79d4dd67e4889087d3673c6da/django/core/handlers/base.py#L30-L35) is pretty lightweight) and call the method or just copy the condition and apply `@transaction.atomic()`. I see both downsides and upsides for either of these options: condition may change in future versions of Django (though `git blame` shows that it hasn't changed since its introduction in 1.6) but the method is undocumented and can also be removed or altered in the future. I'd personally go with calling the method because if it gets removed or its signature changes and the call fails, breakage will be easily noticed and fixed.

The only potential problem is compatibility with pre-1.6 versions of Django. The logic would have to be conditional to account for that (before 1.6 atomic requests were the default behavior and the transaction API was different). Or you could simply state in README that this only works in Django 1.6 and later and earlier versions require a transaction decorator (whatever it was called in earlier versions).

I've had the same problem on an Oracle database with ~300k records and the `recover deleted` button would load for more than 30 minutes without a result. 

The faster query introduced with v2.0.11 only needs to be adjusted slightly in order to support Oracle databases, see pull request #691. Basically, the `AS` in the `FROM` clause needs to be removed, since this is illegal on Oracle but optional on SQLite or PostgreSQL (see [this StackOverflow answer](https://stackoverflow.com/a/21145075)).

Using the faster query, the request now takes about 1 second to complete.

Please let me know if I can do any other testing using an Oracle database.

`face_location` is just the x/y co-ordinates for where the face is located in the image. It's just four numbers - the top, bottom, left and right sides of a box around the face.

`face_encodings` gives you a numerical representation of the structure of a face. This is the only thing you can compare with `compare_faces`. 

They are totally separate things. You can't mix and match them. 

Comparing the face locations with `compare_faces` doesn't make any sense because all you would be comparing is where the faces appeared in the image, not anything about the structure of the faces themselves.

use this tutorial for more details
https://www.pyimagesearch.com/2018/06/18/face-recognition-with-opencv-python-and-deep-learning/

> What prompted this?

There wasn’t a single cause for the changes or I can no longer remember what it was.
But I made the changes. I think they are good and stand by them.

> How is this better?

Before remote tests were more exhaustive than local tests. i.e.: local tests could pass while remote tests failed. With these changes the steps for local tests are the same as for remote tests. 
Devs are able to test on all relevant environments before pushing to remote. And during development it is possible to run only one set of tests for the specific environment the changes affect.
When Python 3.6 is released it will be added to `tox.ini` along with one line to `.travis.yml`.
There is an environment solely for code style. In the future you can add environments for doc style and security.

> Do developers need to run all environment permutations all the time?

No. Developers can run tests against only one environment or a small set of environments. e.g.: `tox -e py35-requests-2.1.0`
[More info on tox docs.](https://tox.readthedocs.io/en/latest/)

> Is Tox the new hotness?

I bet you are seeing something like `405 Method Not Allowed`, which is generated by flask itself (not this extension), and indicating that there is no handler setup for this method. If you want an `OPTION` handler, you would need to add it yourself, along the lines of 

```python
@app.route('/refresh', methods=['OPTION'])
def option_endpoint():
    return 'hello world'
```

I feel like automatically adding `OPTION` endpoints to the flask application is outside of the scope of this extension, and would be better handled by an extension specifically designed to handle this use case (I'm not sure if that extension already exists or not).

I have modified the default behavior of MTimeComparator to catch the error without logging, with the option to provide a logging function. This is a backwards-incompatible change (and would thus require a minor version bump). However, it does not break any existing tests, and we believe it is a more reasonable default than the current behavior of raising an `OSError`. The reasons for this are:

- If a config file disappears inadvertently, we would like to know (hence the logging), but there is no reason it should propagate an error, since it should be able to use the config already loaded into memory.
- If a new version of a program does not need a config file, then we would like to be able to remove that config file at any time so long as we can guarantee that any time the program is run, it will run with the new version. That is not possible if deleting the config file breaks any running old versions.
- If a config file is missing, the error will be identified the next time the program is restarted regardless, so this change does not hide any errors.

If you disagree, I'll change the PR to the minimal behavior you suggested of using a `compare_func`.

So this is the situation as I see it: many people (at least internally, probably also externally?) would say what you implemented is the expected behavior. The swagger spec is unclear about it. For jsonschema (which swagger is based on) this discussion also comes up every once in a while. They treat "no type specified" as "unknown type, do not apply *any* validation". See e.g. [this github issue](https://github.com/json-schema/json-schema/issues/172) for further context.

If we went that route it would mean that when no type is specified that we should ignore things like "required" lists or even property definitions. That's why forcing you to specify a type makes sense. The question is: do we want this to be explicit (you have to specify one) or implicit (we pick one for you)? Or do we want the jsonschema behavior?

I do want this feature, but I'm concerned about the complication this might introduce to `django-extensions` users. That is, in order for the debugging features to work, `django-extensions` users would presumably have to follow [the installation constraints](https://github.com/tomchristie/django-pdb#installation) of `django-pdb`:

- Add to `INSTALLED_APPS` "towards the beginning" in Django>=1.7, otherwise "towards the end".
- Add to `MIDDLEWARE_CLASSES` "after all other middleware".

Questions:

- Can we remove the constraints?
  - If so, shouldn't we do this upstream before trying to integrate?
  - If not, are these constraints acceptable in a popular app that otherwise has no such constraints?
    - If so, then there's no problem and I'm wasting everyone's time.
    - If not, would it be acceptable for these features to be "extra" so the debugging features are disabled if users don't install the optional middleware?

The asyncio context inherits from zmq.Context and only overrides the Socket class. In zmq.asyncio

```python
class Context(_zmq.Context):
    """Context for creating asyncio-compatible Sockets"""
    _socket_class = Socket
```
It does not override the instance() method, hence why you see the sugar context object as the method is called on the parent class. It however is the asyncio Context as the socket class is the asyncio one.

A simple test will show. If I change the asyncio Context class as below:

```python
class Context(_zmq.Context):
    """Context for creating asyncio-compatible Sockets"""
    _socket_class = Socket
    
    @classmethod
    def instance(cls, io_threads=1):
```

This is actually different, but related.  Digging into this library and stdlib:

The #143 ticket is that HTML entities are unescaped into unicode, but not encoded back into HTML entities.

The #291 ticket looks to be a bug with certain Python libraries, including the `unescape` function in the Python 2.7.x and 3.5.x standard html parsing libraries.  The HTML5 spec requires named entities to terminate with a semicolon to avoid "fragile constructs".  the standard library functions don't respect the required ";" rule, and `&current` is being interpreted as `&curren` + `t` to create `\xa4t`.  the html5lib and xml.sax.utils libraries both enforce the trailing ';' rule. I haven't been able to pinpoint where this is being incorrectly escaped yet.

The `sitecustomize` is there to fix wrong reporting with multiprocessing. Luigi does some of its tests in a subprocess (server, MapReduce, ...). The issue is that the coverage context is not transfer automatically to the child process, meaning that any coverage done in a child process won't be reported. Further thoughts (from attempting implementation):

1. General structure of the `BaseCorrelationModel` is not necessarily poorly suited to spatial cross-correlation. The main adaptation is the `apply_correlation` method needs adapting such that it is fed with a list of IMTs rather than a single IMT. The critical distinction here is that the full spatial cross-correlation matrix should be constructed and factorised on the first call, and then on subsequent calls the necessary rows/columns for the sites can then be sliced out. This is working for me with imts as a list and residuals (as input) as samples from the standard Normal distribution of dimension `[len(imts) * len(sites), num_events]`:
```python
    def apply_correlation(self, sites, imts, residuals):
        """
        Applies the correlation model to the sites
        """
        if not self.cache["corma"]:
            # For first implementation then get the lower
            # matrix
            self.get_lower_triangle_correlation_matrix(sites.complete, imts)
        nsites = len(sites)
        if len(sites.complete) == nsites:
            # No filtering of sites
            corr_residuals = np.matmul(self.cache["corma"], residuals)
        else:
            # Need to locate indices of correlation matrix corresponding
            # to the selected sites
            idx = np.tile(sites.indices, len(imts))
            lb = 0
            ub = len(sites)
            norig_sites = len(sites.complete)
            for i in range(len(imts)):
                idx[lb:ub] += (i * norig_sites) 
                lb += nsites
                ub += nsites
            corr_residuals = np.matmul(self.cache["corma"][np.ix_(idx, idx)], residuals)
       
        # corr_residuals is 2-D array [Nimts * Nsites, Nevents]
        # need to re-arrange back to 3-D shape
        residuals = np.empty([len(imts), nsites, corr_residuals.shape[1]])
        idx = np.arange(nsites)
        for i in range(len(imts)):
            residuals[i] = corr_residuals[idx, :]
            idx += nsites
        return residuals
``` 

2. Changes are needed in the `GmfComputer` and further upstream. If one were to assume all IMTS can be calculated from a desired GSIM then IMTs can be set upon instantiation of the GmfComputer and the loop over the IMTs pushed further down into `_compute(...)`. In the case of no correlation the output would be identical to that in the current form. The problem comes if one were to need to calculated different IMTs from different GSIMS (e.g. Sa(1.0) from GMPE1, PGV from GMPE 2). Leaving aside theoretical questions about this, there is precedent for this application and in some cases this is the only feasible approach.  In this case it is better if the user defines not just a GMPE but a dictionary associating GMPEs to IMTs (or vice-versa), e.g.:
```xml
<logicTreeBranch branchID="ASB14+T03">
    <uncertaintyModel>{AkkarEtAlRjb2014: [PGA, PGV], TravasarouEtAl2003: [IA]}</uncertaintyModel>
    <uncertaintyWeight>1.0</uncertaintyWeight>
</logicTreeBranch>
```
In this case the GSIMs and their corresponding IMTs can be kept. Many upstream changes needed in the calculators.

```python
from pgpy.packet.packets import UserAttribute
from pgpy.packet.subpackets import Image
import os
import time

def gen_userattr(num_images):
    ua = UserAttribute()
    for _ in range(num_images):
        i = Image()
        i.encoding = 0x0
        i.image = bytearray(os.urandom(8192))
        ua.subpackets['Image'] = i
    ua.update_hlen()
    return ua

def gen_data(nb, ni):
    last = start = time.time()
    buf = bytearray(bytes(gen_userattr(ni)))
    count = nb // len(buf)
    for _ in range(count):
        buf += bytearray(bytes(gen_userattr(ni)))
        if time.time() - last > 10:
            print("buflen = {:d} / {:d}".format(len(buf), nb))
            last = time.time()
    return buf

for num_images in [0, 1, 2]:
    print("generating test data with {:d} images".format(num_images))
    data = bytearray()
    for num_bytes in [(100 * 1024), (1024 ** 2), (15 * 1024 ** 2)]:
        nb = num_bytes - len(data)
        print("generating {:d} more bytes...".format(nb))
        data += gen_data(nb, num_images)
        print("writing...")
        with open('t_{:d}_{:d}'.format(num_images, num_bytes // 1024), 'wb') as tf:
            tf.write(data)
```

To follow up on this: 

My issue persisted for a long time, and I never got to the bottom of it. I learned that the problem is specific to OS X El Capitan (10.11), and that it seems to be related to how `git fetch --verbose` writes to stderr, and the capturing of that output. It can be reproduced in El Cap by running the following commands within a repo that has many branches: 

```bash
# this will output a complete list of refs
git fetch --verbose 2>&1
# this will mysteriously output a truncated list of refs
git fetch --verbose 2>&1 | cat
```

I could reproduce the issue regardless of the version of git installed in El Cap (I tested the Mac system version, homebrew's version, and the latest release compiled from source).

Anyway, the good news is that while I never figured out the root cause of the truncated output, I am pleased to report that it looks like GitPython no longer throws an AssertionError as a result of it, probably due to the fix in https://github.com/gitpython-developers/GitPython/issues/442. I have not had a problem since upgrading to GitPython 2.0.6. Which is a huge relief, because I was stumped at how to further troubleshoot the issue with git. :)

That is accurate. Trust chains are not applicable in this case, as you are explicitly passing the issuer cert to verify the signature against (which is already marked as trusted). Expiration time is in this case ignored, much due to the fact that it is under-specified how it should be handled, as attestation certificates cannot be updated.

The U2F specification has nothing to say on the subject of attestation verification. The implementation in this library is one lightweight approach, which assumes you have some method of vetting which CAs to trust, and for how long.

Hi @robwhess: I do get notified on every setuptools message, so no need to mention me explicitly, but never hurts if you're unsure.

I'm pretty sure this issue is related to #250 and /pypa/pip#3. I'm not familiar with what `python setup.py nosetests` does, but I suspect it's similar to what `setup.py test` or `setup.py pytest` (from [pytest-runner](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/pytest-runner)) do: installs packages using easy_install to `./.eggs` and link them into the environment before running tests.

> I've had problems using setuptools with namespace packages in the past, too. For example, even using python setup.py install with multiple namespace packages doesn't seem to work, and it's become my common practice to instead run something like python setup.py sdist && pip install dist/*.

This is definitely linked to pypa/pip#3. A few notes:

- When you invoke `python setup.py install`, the behavior is not obvious. If the setup.py imports setuptools, the package will be installed as an egg using easy_install, which creates a multi-homed package, something which pip doesn't support well, and which [I've attempted to document here](/pypa/python-packaging-user-guide/pull/159).
- If setup.py does not import setuptools but instead invokes distutils, the package will be installed in a flat form, similar to how pip installs them, but until Python 3.3 and PEP 420 namespace support, that namespace package can only exist in one location.
- When you invoke `python setup.py sdist && pip install dist/*`, that's effectively `pip install .`, and effectively forces the flat, non-egg installation even if setuptools is present in the setup.py.

Only in the first case will eggs installed in `./.eggs` be able to co-exist with packages installed via pip or distutils (when a namespace is shared).

There is one topic I forgot, and I also don't feel it really important:
the input range for float images.
Should it be [-1,1] or [0,1]? 

I don't think so. The algorithm itself as absolutely linear, it makes no difference if we scale or shift the values. It is a bit similar to the color spaces: if there is a universal approach, then let's use it.
But if we enforce [0,1] or [-1,1], then should we enforce these for the output range too?

E.g. it is possible that someone wants radius=1 and amount=10 which will generate overshoot.
Should the user scale down the image into [-0.1,0.1] to guarantee that after USM the result will still be in [-1,1]? Or should we just ignore the output range? Or make a "clip" parameter? 

[0,1] or [-1,1]? In most color spaces there are no negative values but there are exceptions, e.g. for X-ray applications in medical imaging.

So should we enforce an arbitrary limit just for fun, give a warning (e.g. "in skimage the recommended data range is [0,1] for float-type images "), or should we just ignore this question for now, and add warning/assert when the question is settled?

I don't see any user who would use the filter and for whom it is easier to transform the image to the 0,1 range, ask the filter for clipping, and transform it back to the original range, than just clipping the result if he/she really wants it. So I think it would make the filter less convenient to use, and it doesn't help the filter implementation, accuracy, speed or any other aspect.

So I would vote for the current version, but I am open to other solutions.

I'm not saying the ~~magic~~ content in the `__init__.py` is wrong, not at all.

Only one `__init__.py` will ever be executed (the others will be scanned, but their contents will not be executed). That's the case already.

The warnings happen because Python (or setuptools, whatever) notices that the `vsc/__init__.py` files are present in multiple different places on the filesystem (i.e. in different `.eggs` subdirectories).

If we register `vsc` as a namespace package in the `setup.py`, we're just telling Python that's exactly what we want to do, and it will stop warning about this.

Note that we get basically the same warning in the case where `vsc-base` is available both in the EasyBuild installation prefix *and* on the OS (there it *is* wrong, and the warning is legitimate)
We'll need to check whether the warning is still going to occur in that case. We would want that, I think...

> As told before, it's not hard to implement missing parts of discovery and access (3 and 3).
But there is one more missing bit. What should we do if after a long time all endpoints of etcd cluster will become not accessible? If we know that we are connecting via proxy or load-balancer, we should just continue trying. But if we are connecting to all etcd nodes directly, it is getting though, because we need to "re-run" initial discovery:

I don't think it's unreasonable to ask users to manage the state of their configuration file if they are entering a static list of addresses.  This should be clearly stated in the documentation.   What does Patroni do when it's unable to connect to the single endpoint after a long time?  Is there any issue in continuing with the existing approach, which I think is error and retry indefinitely?  

> Currently, if you have only one host used for initial discovery, you have to update the configuration file only when that host is moved. If you specify more than one host there, you'll have to update the configuration file when each of those hosts specified in the configuration file is moved (otherwise, you'll eventually end up with a list of non-existing hosts). In other words, you still need a sync process, but you'll end up doing more work if you sync multiple (presumably all) hosts instead of one. I don't really see any benefits to that.

This would only be a problem if you move nodes super often and don't have any automation surrounding this process.

It seems the provided wheel file is not packaged correctly:
1. The `data` directory from the repo is completely missing, but it contains the images, glade and schema.
2. Furthermore, the wheel file contains only `paths.py.in`, which obviously needs some processing to become `paths.py`. That's why python raises the exception `ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'guake.paths'`

I copied `data` to my machine, updated the `*_DIR` variables in `paths.py.in` accordingly and renamed it to `paths.py`. After these fixes I was able to start guake.
When copying `data` to `~/.local/lib/<python_version>/site-packages/guake`, `~/.local/lib/<python_version>/site-packages/guake/paths.py` shall look like this:
```
import os

LOCALE_DIR = "/usr/local/share/locale"
IMAGE_DIR = "/home/<user>/.local/lib/<python_version>/site-packages/guake/data/pixmaps"
GLADE_DIR = "/home/<user>/.local/lib/<python_version>/site-packages/guake/data"
SCHEMA_DIR = "/home/<user>/.local/lib/<python_version>/site-packages/guake/data"
GUAKE_THEME_DIR = ""
LOGIN_DESTOP_PATH = ""
AUTOSTART_FOLDER = ""
```


Please add `data` to the wheel file and let the setup mechanism process `paths.py.in`.

    ```python
    storage.set_defaults(foo='bar', connection=cnxn, ...)
    ```

This error is a security related message: it happens when you try to create
a CloudFormation stack that includes the creation of IAM related resources.
You have to explicitly tell CloudFormation that you are OK with that.

To make it work, simply add the parameter --capabilities CAPABILITY_IAM to
your deploy command. That should solve the problem.

Here's all the code, interestingly if you render the frames as 128x128 or 256x256 it works, but even 2 frames with 512x512 breaks it.

```
import numpy as np
from PIL import Image
import imageio


def quadratic(a, b, c):
    t = b*b-4*a*c
    if t < 0:
        return np.array([])
    if t < 1E-3:
        return np.array([-b/(2*a)])
    t = np.sqrt(t)
    if b < 0:
        t = -0.5*(b-t)
    else:
        t = -0.5*(b+t)
    t0 = t/a
    t1 = c/t
    if t0 < t1:
        return np.array([t0, t1])
    return np.array([t1, t0])


def ray_sphere_intersection(ro, rd, radius):
    a = np.dot(rd, rd)
    b = 2*np.dot(ro, rd)
    c = np.dot(ro, ro) - radius*radius
    return quadratic(a, b, c)


class Atmosphere:
    def __init__(self, radius_earth, radius_atmosphere,
                        height_rayleigh, height_mie,
                        beta_rayleigh, beta_mie,
                        sun_direction, n_samples_sight, n_samples_light):
        self.radius_earth = radius_earth
        self.radius_atmosphere = radius_atmosphere
        self.height_rayleigh = height_rayleigh
        self.height_mie = height_mie
        self.beta_rayleigh = beta_rayleigh
        self.beta_mie = beta_mie
        self.sun_direction = sun_direction
        self.n_samples_sight = n_samples_sight
        self.n_samples_light = n_samples_light

    def get_color(self, ro, rd):

        # find the intersection of the ray with the outer shell of the atmosphere
        # here we use the full ray in case one is viewing from outside the atmosphere
        ts = ray_sphere_intersection(ro, rd, self.radius_atmosphere)
        if len(ts) == 0 or len(ts) == 1 or ts[1] < 0:
            return np.array([0.0, 0.0, 0.0])
        if ts[0] < 0.0:
            ts[0] = 0.0
        sls = (ts[1]-ts[0])/self.n_samples_sight  # segment length sight

        # prepare variables for physics calculations
        odsr = 0.0  # optical depth sight rayleigh
        odsm = 0.0  # optical depth sight mie
        sum_rayleigh = np.array([0.0, 0.0, 0.0])
        sum_mie = np.array([0.0, 0.0, 0.0])
        for i in range(self.n_samples_sight):
            rts = ro + (i+0.5)*sls*rd
            hs = np.linalg.norm(rts) - self.radius_earth
            hr = np.exp(-hs/self.height_rayleigh)*sls
            hm = np.exp(-hs/self.height_mie)*sls
            odsr += hr
            odsm += hm

            tl = ray_sphere_intersection(rts, self.sun_direction, self.radius_atmosphere)
            sll = tl[1] / n_samples_light  # segment length light
            odlr = 0.0  # optical depth light rayleigh
            odlm = 0.0  # optical depth light mie
            for j in range(self.n_samples_light):
                rtl = rts + (j+0.5)*sll*self.sun_direction
                hl = np.linalg.norm(rtl) - self.radius_earth
                odlr += np.exp(-hl/height_rayleigh)*sll
                odlm += np.exp(-hl/height_mie)*sll

            tau = beta_rayleigh*(odsr + odlr) + beta_mie*1.1*(odsm + odlm)
            attenuation = np.exp(-tau)
            sum_rayleigh += hr*attenuation
            sum_mie += hm*attenuation

        mu = np.dot(rd, self.sun_direction)
        phase_r = 3.0/(16*np.pi)*(1.0+mu*mu)
        g = 0.76
        phase_m = 3.0/(8.0*np.pi)*((1.0 - g*g)*(1.0 + mu*mu)) / ((2.0 + g*g)*((1.0 + g*g - 2.0*g*mu)**1.5))
        return (sum_rayleigh*beta_rayleigh*phase_r + sum_mie*beta_mie*phase_m)*20








def spherical_to_rectangular(phi, theta):
    return np.array([np.sin(theta)*np.cos(phi),
                     np.cos(theta),
                     np.sin(theta)*np.sin(phi)])


def tonemap(v):
    if v < 1.413:
        return (v*0.38317)**(1.0/2.2)
    return 1-np.exp(-v)

def clamp(v):
    v *= 255.0
    if v < 0:
        return 0
    if v > 255:
        return 255
    return int(v)

def interpolate(a, b, t):
    return (1-t)*a + t*b

def render_fisheye(atmosphere, img):
    img_width, img_height = img.size
    pixels = img.load()
    for i in range(img_width):
        x = 2.0*(i+0.5)/(img_width-1.0)-1.0
        for j in range(img_height):
            y = 2*(j+0.5)/(img_height-1.0)-1.0
            z2 = x*x + y*y
            if z2 <= 1.0:
                phi = np.arctan2(y, x)
                theta = np.arccos(1-z2)
                ro = np.array([0, atmosphere.radius_earth+1, 0])
                rd = spherical_to_rectangular(phi, theta)
                c = atmosphere.get_color(ro, rd)
                # c[0] = tonemap(c[0])
                # c[1] = tonemap(c[1])
                # c[2] = tonemap(c[2])
                pixels[i,j] = (clamp(c[0]), clamp(c[1]), clamp(c[2]))
        print(float(i)/img_width*100)


def render_perspective(atmosphere, img):
    return None

if __name__ == '__main__':
    radius_earth = 6360e3
    radius_atmosphere = 6420e3
    height_rayleigh = 7994.0
    height_mie = 1200.0
    beta_rayleigh = np.array([3.8e-6, 13.5e-6, 33.1e-6])
    beta_mie = np.array([21e-6, 21e-6, 21e-6])
    angle = np.pi/2.5
    sun_direction = np.array([0.0, 1.0, 0.0])
    n_samples_sight = 16
    n_samples_light = 8
    atmosphere = Atmosphere(radius_earth, radius_atmosphere,
                            height_rayleigh, height_mie,
                            beta_rayleigh, beta_mie, sun_direction,
                            n_samples_sight, n_samples_light)

    # img = Image.new('RGB', (128, 128), 0)
    # render_fisheye(atmosphere, img)
    # img.show()

    n_images = 2
    for i in range(n_images):
        t = i/(n_images - 1.0)
        angle = interpolate(-np.pi/2.0, np.pi/2.0, t)
        atmosphere.sun_direction = np.array([0.0, np.cos(angle), -np.sin(angle)])
        img = Image.new('RGB', (512, 512), 0)
        render_fisheye(atmosphere, img)
        img.save("atmosphere"+str(i)+".jpg")

    images = []
    for i in range(n_images):
        images.append(imageio.imread("atmosphere"+str(i)+".jpg"))
    # kargs = {'duration': 5}
    # imageio.mimsave('atmosphere.gif', np.array(images), 'GIF', **kargs)
    imageio.mimsave('atmosphere.gif', images)
```

I would encourage Autobahn to use `optionsForClientTLS(...)` as a sensible default when a contextFactory is not otherwise provided. The problem is that it would be a breaking change for users with a deficient local ca-store, so it is entirely _inappropriate_ to bring that in by the back door with this change. 

While the diff shows I added `ssl.ClientContextFactory()` it is simply existing behavior being relocated up a few lines.

For the "built in" checks, I believe I am treating it as a triple that must match -- my question is more regarding the API presented via ``ConnectionRequest`` to ``onConnect``. For example, we could maintain compatibility by leaving ``.origin`` as a string with the host, and add ``.scheme`` and ``.port`` or similar -- *or* force everyone to (possibly) change existing code and make ``.origin`` a ``(scheme, host, port)`` triple.

Certainly the latter seems "more correct", but might not be worth breaking code. I guess a "third way" would be to add a new ``.websocket_origin`` as a triple, deprecate ``.origin`` (removing ``.origin`` entirely at some point).

If it delays the nginx response that's a bug. It's supposed to be non-blocking. So the first request each router instance responds to gets returned without a stapled ocsp even though the flag is turned on. Unless there is a bug, nginx continues to respond without a stapled ocsp until it receives one from the certificate authority. And when the stapled ocsp is missing or invalid the browser takes care of querying the endpoint specified by the cert itself. And depending on the browser _it_ can block until it receives a valid response but nginx shouldn't.

A note on colorized output, if we decide to use it at all:
`ls` detects whether or not it is writing to a terminal before colorizing its output, so that it doesn't blurt out ANSI color codes when used in a pipeline. I think we would need to use the same behavior, or people will likely be surprised (read: dismayed) by the results.

We could also use `LS_COLORS` to determine correct colorization, but it's a bit of a pain to parse.

---

I think we can decide to strictly not follow symlinks when doing a recursive `ls`. The GNU `ls` tool doesn't support it -- why should we, right?
That is, `ls -R some_link/` works, but it doesn't follow any dir symlinks it encounters.

That aside, I agree that we want to deal with #46 and the potential SDK support for recursive `ls` before doing anything here.

---

`globus symlink [OPTIONS] ENDPOINT_ID:SOURCE_PATH TARGET_PATH` makes me a little bit nervous, as it's odd to require the endpoint with only one of the two paths. I'm not sure that there's a phrasing of it that isn't confusing, but a couple of notes:

- It's probably worth following the convention set by `ln` and providing the link target, followed by the link path. Personally, I always find this ordering counterintuitive, but I think it would be _more_ confusing to do the opposite, since everyone knows `ln`
- It may be better to not use the `ENDPOINT:PATH` notation at all for this, and make `ENDPOINT TARGET LINK_PATH` the args
- We _could_ add a version of `EndpointPlusPath` params where the endpoint is optional, behaving the same way as the optpath version does, and require that we get at least one endpoint and that if there are 2 endpoints, they must match. I'm not sure that this would be a good idea

So, looking for the reason as to why ase._client would be None, I found the ase._client yielded by `_generate_destination_for_source` in `blobxfer/operations/upload.py` was None, because `populate_from_local` didn't assign the correct client to ase._client, as the mode passed "auto" (string) wasn't recognized as `StorageModes.Auto`.

So, in the end, it was a really silly oversight on my part and used `mode=StorageModes.Auto` when creating the UploadOptions.

Thanks for the question and the comments. 

You have to understand that one of the limitation of Jupyter notebook is that it runs within a browser, thus there are limitation:
  - Not complete API to do copy past. 
  - Some shortcuts that can't be take (for example we can't intercept Ctrl-W to close tab. ...etc.
We thus have to make compromises. Hopefully the web api and browser implementation eveolve so we can push thing further with time. 

You will be happy to learn that since this issue was opened, new copy-past API have been developed and that – if you are on a system that have these APIs – you should be able use regular copy past, and there are even newer API that should make things works better – you are welcome to send PRs to use them.
Now for other users, we do have to provide solutions.

For the edit-command mode, let's put aside the fact that a lot of people like it and are efficient with it we have a coule of data points and historical references:
  -  initial versions of Jupyter (when it was till IPython) used to not have a non modal system, it was way clunkier than what we have now.
  - We are cornered by the limitation of the web API to get keyboard shortcuts. My guess is that it's not the modality that bother you, but that you actually need to be in command mode for most.
  - Many interfaces are modal, you just don't realize it, like while writing this, if I press Ctrl-F and type thing it's suddenly not writing in this text area.


Now, most of what I've said above has already been said earlier in the thread, and there are [other frontends](https://nteract.io/) with different restrictions, and this is an open-source software, provided for free. You are welcome to contribute to it. If you can do better, than that would improve your workflow as well as other peoples ! That would be a win-win situation !

Sure. The `KeyMaterial` object is a structure containing the key bytes along with other key metadata. If you just want the bytes of the key value, do the following:

```
key = client.get(...)
key_bytes = key.key_block.key_value.key_material.value
```

However, the demo you're looking at uses the old `KMIPProxy` client. I'd recommend using the newer `ProxyKmipClient` client. Getting the key bytes is much easier:

```
from kmip import pie
client = pie.ProxyKmipClient(...)
...
key = client.get(...)
key_bytes = key.value
```

`synonymous = v.type == 'p' and str(v.posedit.edit) == "="`

For example:
```
>>> var_c = hp.parse_hgvs_variant("NM_006158.4:c.1416T>C")
>>> var_p = am38.c_to_p(var_c)
>>> var_p.posedit
PosEdit(pos=Ser472, edit==, uncertain=True)
>>> synonymous = var_p.type == 'p' and str(var_p.posedit.edit) == "="
>>> synonymous
True
```

Also, note that a parsed synonymous variant (as opposed to one generated by projection) has the same structure: 
```
>>> var_p2 = hp.parse_hgvs_variant(str(var_p))
>>> var_p2.posedit
PosEdit(pos=Ser472, edit==, uncertain=True)
```

We have updated several issues with this previously, but usually these issues boil down to numerical inaccuracies that are next to impossible to prevent.  The main reason is that the boosted trees model often uses values from the training dataset in determining the split points.  At these specific values, calling the models at these specific values can often give a different prediction.  

The reasons for this are usually due to two things. First, the training is done with float32, but predictions in CoreML are done with float64, and minor differences in the numerical values cause the model to go down different branches, and these different branches will have different predictions.  Second, converting from decimal values to float32 or float64 (e.g. reading from a CSV file, giving it explicitly, etc.) will produce slightly different numbers.  In other words, each of the following routes can be a different number:  decimal->float64, decimal->float32, decimal->float64->float32, float64->float32, etc.  As a result, if the original value was present in the training data, each of these paths may give a different prediction.  Thus, when testing boosted tree model export, it's very difficult to use values from the training data.

We ran into similar errors. We deployed a fix which was merged into Django upstream by @edmorley () and for a PR is currently open in this library () to stop closing connections on every request (turn `close()` into a no-op for the `Pylibmc` backend). We hit lots of connection errors when we first deployed and had to revert.

After investigation, I realized the errors were only actually happening on our Celery worker instances. The problem is that Celery's default worker class (prefork) spins up new workers using fork, which copies all the memory/open sockets/file descriptors of the parent process into each subprocess. This is done to save memory, but there's a subtle problem: Celery initializes the Django application in the master process before forking. At least in our case, the initialization code calls into Memcache which means it establishes the client connection object before the app is fully initialized, which means each subprocess is sharing the underlying `pylibmc.Client` object.

Because of this, each worker process will attempt to use the same set of sockets as the master process, which I believe is the cause of the connection errors (`libmemcache`, the underlying C library, [is neither thread nor process safe](http://docs.libmemcached.org/libmemcached.html#threads-and-processes)).

We tried to fix this by hooking into Celery's worker_process_init to do the proper actions:
```python
def disconnect_default_cache_on_worker_fork(*args, **kwargs):
    from django.core.cache import cache
    cache._cache.disconnect_all()
```

This seemed to work for a short time, but then we starting seeing the `Transport endpoint is not connected` errors described in this PR when the Celery workers forked and initialized (one of these errors per worker per initialization, it seems), as well as `Timed out waiting for UP message`. These errors seem related to the fact that the `pylibmc.Client` object is not thread safe, yet all the Celery workers are sharing them. These issues seem similar to those discussed here:
https://github.com/lericson/pylibmc/issues/150

So I think it's a problem with Celery using prefork workers. I'm not sure what the right solution is. Probably we will try to run a different Celery worker type (eventlet or greenlet) or make some attempt to use a threadsafe version of `pylibmc.Client` (maybe one of the pools).

Intersting Python 2/3 difference here: in Python 3, the `keys()` method of an `OrderedDict` returns a `KeysView`, not a list. This is now what `get_params()` returns, and this causes a bunch of problems.

The easiest fix is just to wrap it in `list(...)` to make sure `get_params()` returns a list in both Python 2 and Python 3. I'm okay with that solution, but before doing that I figured I'd ask if there are any caveats here, or if there is maybe a better solution. Hi Matt,

The logic behind the change in Local_GangaMC_Service.py is to avoid loading every single job just to check the backend object associated with it.
(I should probably add a test to see if a backend has already been 'setup' or not. This is supposed to be run once per backend (or at least that's my understanding).)

The logic for this is for large repos (many hundreds of master jobs) running through the loading of each of these for the backend object is quite an expensive operation even when the subjobs aren't loaded compared to the fact that the smaller index files for the jobs have already been loaded at this point.
I would also like to avoid loading the jobs due to any hidden overheads that there may be such as memory footprint.

I know it's not ideal but I'm thinking of expanding the cache data per job in the future so that using this doesn't require something like an eval in order to be used.

I'm for the changes in Job if they don't cause a slowdown but again I don't really want to trigger the loading of Jobs from disk just to remove them. The backend may not expose the remove function through the GPI as it's just the same as the dummy remove function in the IBackend. If this hasn't been exposed I think loading the job just to call the dummy function is potentially bad.
Same goes for the Application and 'transition_update'.

You can get hardware timestamp using `frame::get_timestamp()` method. Please make sure `frame::get_frame_timestamp_domain()` returns `RS2_TIMESTAMP_DOMAIN_HARDWARE_CLOCK`.
You can also fetch host time the frame reached librealsense using `frame.get_frame_metadata(RS2_FRAME_METADATA_TIME_OF_ARRIVAL)` and when it reached the driver (Media Foundation / V4L2) using `frame.get_frame_metadata(RS2_FRAME_METADATA_BACKEND_TIMESTAMP)`. 
Please note that frame timestamp is not comparable to the later two, since there is no clock synchronisation between camera and host clocks.

@syrusakbary We have been looking a lot into a parallel middleware in our app (on Python 2.7). I was planning on open sourcing them, but the one we currently use is a simple ThreadPool middleware (the gevent middleware turned out not to be compatible with our app because we use grpc). That has its own issues though because it actually is spinning off threads. So our latest approach would be to use Futures (grpc is also using futures), which has a nice backport to python 2.7: https://github.com/agronholm/pythonfutures as well with an additional package to make it then-able: https://github.com/dvdotsenko/python-future-then

Apart from the fact that this would be neater for our app (so I will already be slightly more biased towards a futures solution), I also do think it is the more pythonic solution and more and more projects (like grpc) do actually implement the futures interface. So this would in my opinion, generally make it easier to integrate with the parallel middleware.

Since this is something that we need in our app and it has been on our internal TODO, that might be something that I, or possibly one of my colleagues could tackle. But the earliest for me would be sometime towards end of next week.

```python
from database.operation import Operation
import api_utils
import batch_schema
import graphene
import logging

# Load logging configuration
log = logging.getLogger(__name__)


class CreateBatchOwnerInput(graphene.InputObjectType):
    """Arguments to create a batch owner."""
    name = graphene.String(required=True)


class CreateBatchOwner(graphene.Mutation):
    """Mutation to create a batch owner."""
    # Declare class attributes
    batch_owner = graphene.Field(batch_schema.BatchOwner)

    class Arguments:
        input = CreateBatchOwnerInput(required=True)

    def mutate(self, info, input):
        """Mutation to create a batch owner."""
        # Convert input to dictionary
        data = api_utils.input_to_dictionary(input)
        batch_owner = Operation('BatchOwner').create(**data)
        return CreateBatchOwner(batch_owner=batch_owner)
```

Certbot already uses entry points for discovering and accessing plugins (see, e.g., [disco.py#L194](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/master/certbot/plugins/disco.py#L194)).

I think that pattern works well, and is sufficient for, traditional distribution mechanisms. However, there's a bit lacking when it comes to plugins with `certbot-auto`. 

As you know, `certbot-auto` automatically and continually updates Certbot itself (i.e., `acme`, the core `certbot` package, and "first class" plugins like `certbot-apache` and `certbot-nginx`), but doesn't do anything for other plugins (e.g., "second class" first-party plugins like the DNS authenticators or third-party plugins).

It seems like we need _some_ additional logic to ensure that these other plugins are kept up to date (in a way that ensures their integrity, like `pipstrap` would if they were managed by `certbot-auto`).

It also seems like we want to persist some record of the plugin outside of the `venv`. It seems like preserving the `venv` can be problematic in some cases (#4047) and being able to safely remove the `venv` and start from a "clean slate" seems useful to rule out an entire class of potential issues when debugging failures.

It seems like the idea solution would be one that:

1. Keeps plugins up-to-date.
2. Guarantees integrity of plugins.
3. Allows the `venv` to be deleted.
4. Introduces minimal additional complexity.
5. Does not rely on OS-specific patterns.

If we can't achieve all of these, should we prioritize the list?

The bug is that the plugin logic is simply doing a search on the `nginx.conf` file, rather than parsing it and expanding any `include` directives.

Whilst moving everything to `nginx.conf` might be a workaround, that breaks the whole model of packaged versions of nginx, whereby the distribution (in this case Debian or Ubuntu) ships a default file and then you override any directives in different files. If you edit `nginx.conf` instead, you risk getting a configuration conflict each time nginx is updated, because the package manager will notice that your configuration differs from the one supplied by the package (this isn't unique to nginx, it happens with PHP and lots of other software).

OK, changes in the sos core is mostly done, at least all tests pass, so you can start testing the new syntax. Basically, you will need to 

1. Upgrade to Python 3.6
2. Use format string instead of SoS string, namely changing

```
output: "${input!b}.html"
```
to
```
output: f"{input:b}.html"
```
3. Use `{ }` instead of `${ }` in scripts, or use option `sigil='${ }'`.
4. Use `{}` instead of `${ }` in `.hosts.yml` files
4. Section option `sigil` is removed.

I tried to made the change as small as possible so the internal structure such as DAGs are untouched. There should be room for further performance improvement but that is not the priority now. Update on other sos modules should be relatively easy, but there is huge amount of work ahead to update all the documentations and examples, so the lesson is that we should have made the decision last year.

@layeoh That's a pretty impressive first driver!

Regarding the handling of logging messages, I would recommend the following:

  * Delete the setting of logger level in the driver (no surprises here!)
  * Do the following (or something very similar in your scripts/notebooks):

```python
logger = logging.getLogger('<name-of-driver-module>')  # the name is probably 'qcodes/instrument_drivers/cryogenic/CryogenicSMS120C'
handler = logging.StreamHandler(sys.stdout)
logger.setLevel(logging.INFO)  # set the logger's level
handler.setLevel(logging.INFO)  # here you get a second chance to discriminate
formatter = logging.Formatter('%(asctime)s - %(name)s - %(levelname)s - %(message)s')
handler.setFormatter(formatter)
logger.addHandler(handler)
```

It's probably a bit longer than what you are doing now, but I think that this is more correct. It's also easy to modify to log to a file plus you get to set the format which can be extremely helpful, e.g. by adding information on which function is producing the message (check out https://docs.python.org/3/library/logging.html#logrecord-attributes to see what' spossible).

I have the impression that `skimage` uses a wrapper around `PIL`/`matplotlib` for image loading / saving, and all the filters and operations are written using numpy. This might mean that it's probably slower than using `PIL`, which has C code (and which is way slower than OpenCV).
Also, it's probably not as widely available as PIL.

I indeed think that passing around PIL images might not be ideal, but note that we can convert it to `numpy.array` or `torch.Tensor` at no cost.
Also, there is `accimage` in the pytorch folder, that provides some optimized functions for image transformation.

More importantly, I think that we should have a standard for image loading / processing, so that all the models are interchangeable without problems. So HxWxC vs CxHxW, RGB vs BGR, [0,1] vs [0,255], etc.
Using PIL is a way of keeping a standard, so it's a good thing.

you could just reuse the `Streamlink` class 

**EXAMPLE** of your script

used `values` (python3) in this example for `intervalues`

```py
from streamlink.plugin import api
from streamlink import plugins

_loaded_plugins = None


class Streamlink2(Streamlink):

    def load_builtin_plugins(self):
        global _loaded_plugins
        print("-----")
        if not _loaded_plugins:
            print(_loaded_plugins)
        else:
            print(len(_loaded_plugins))
        if _loaded_plugins and True:  # self.get_option("no-plugin-reload")
            self._update_loaded_plugins()
        else:
            self.load_plugins(plugins.__path__[0])
            if True:  # self.get_option("no-plugin-reload")
                _loaded_plugins = self.plugins.copy()
        if not _loaded_plugins:
            print(_loaded_plugins)
        else:
            print(len(_loaded_plugins))

    def _update_loaded_plugins(self):
        # Set the global http session
        api.http = self.http
        # Reload plugins from saved ones
        self.plugins = _loaded_plugins.copy()
        # Update plugins session
        for plugin in self.plugins.values():
            plugin.session = self
```

Yeah, you'll want to use an `async_generator`-based AG, instead of a native one.

`async_generator` had some messy problems like this – it used to use `pytest-asyncio` to test things, but then `pytest-asyncio` itself gained a dependency on `async_generator` and it messed up our coverage and things.

The way we fixed it there was to switch to using a silly little fake async loop: https://github.com/python-trio/async_generator/blob/master/async_generator/_tests/conftest.py

Basically that `conftest.py` does two things:

* It defines a `mock_sleep` routine (maybe this should have been called `checkpoint`) and a tiny coroutine runner where the only primitive async calls it knows how to handle are calls to `mock_sleep`

* It arranges to use this runner for any tests that are marked as `async`

I think that might work for outcome as well? outcome's async tests are pretty simple.

```python

class GroupConv2d(Layer):
    """The :class:`GroupConv2d` class is 2D grouped convolution, see `here <https://blog.yani.io/filter-group-tutorial/>`__.

    Parameters
    --------------
    layer : :class:`Layer`
        Previous layer.
    n_filter : int
        The number of filters.
    filter_size : int
        The filter size.
    stride : int
        The stride step.
    n_group : int
        The number of groups.
    act : activation function
        The activation function of this layer.
    padding : str
        The padding algorithm type: "SAME" or "VALID".
    W_init : initializer
        The initializer for the weight matrix.
    b_init : initializer or None
        The initializer for the bias vector. If None, skip biases.
    W_init_args : dictionary
        The arguments for the weight matrix initializer.
    b_init_args : dictionary
        The arguments for the bias vector initializer.
    name : str
        A unique layer name.
    """

    def __init__(
            self,
            layer=None,
            n_filter=32,
            filter_size=(3, 3),
            strides=(2, 2),
            n_group=2,
            act=tf.identity,
            padding='SAME',
            W_init=tf.truncated_normal_initializer(stddev=0.02),
            b_init=tf.constant_initializer(value=0.0),
            W_init_args=None,
            b_init_args=None,
            name='groupconv',
    ):  # Windaway
        if W_init_args is None:
            W_init_args = {}
        if b_init_args is None:
            b_init_args = {}

        Layer.__init__(self, name=name)
        self.inputs = layer.outputs
        groupConv = lambda i, k: tf.nn.conv2d(i, k, strides=[1, strides[0], strides[1], 1], padding=padding)
        channels = int(self.inputs.get_shape()[-1])
        with tf.variable_scope(name) as vs:
            We = tf.get_variable(
                name='weights',
                shape=[filter_size[0], filter_size[1], channels / n_group, n_filter],
                initializer=W_init,
                dtype=D_TYPE,
                trainable=True,
                **W_init_args)
            if b_init:
                bi = tf.get_variable(
                    name='biases', shape=[
                        n_filter,
                    ], initializer=b_init, dtype=D_TYPE, trainable=True, **b_init_args)
        if n_group == 1:
            conv = groupConv(self.inputs, We)
        else:
            inputGroups = tf.split(axis=3, num_or_size_splits=n_group, value=self.inputs)
            weightsGroups = tf.split(axis=3, num_or_size_splits=n_group, value=We)
            convGroups = [groupConv(i, k) for i, k in zip(inputGroups, weightsGroups)]
            conv = tf.concat(axis=3, values=convGroups)
        if b_init is not None:
            conv = tf.add(conv, bi, name='add')

        self.outputs = act(conv)
        self.all_layers = list(layer.all_layers)
        self.all_params = list(layer.all_params)
        self.all_drop = dict(layer.all_drop)
        self.all_layers.extend([self.outputs])
        if b_init is not None:
            self.all_params.extend([We, bi])
        else:
            self.all_params.extend([We])
```

It has, but I think the new 3.1 plan is a major improvement, backing off to something much more "one step at a time".  Once bugs are ironed out, the diff between 2.1 and 3.1 apps will be *very* small, which is significantly different to the original 2.1->3.0 plan, where we had ROUTER/DEALER as legacy socket types that behaved like 2.1, while the rest of the sockets moved forward with a complete redesign.  Now the wire-protocol no longer changes from 2.x to 3.x, and all socket types behave the same, with two major changes:

* PUB/SUB sockets are filtered on the *PUB* side, potentially reducing network load.
* durable sockets are removed (routing a message by IDENTITY to a peer you have seen before, but are not currently connected to, will no longer keep the message buffered in anticipation of a reconnect).

Neither of which should have any effect on 99% of pyzmq application developers, very much unlike 3.0.

The rest of changes are largely C-API adjustments to a bit more clean and POSIX-y, which affects pyzmq itself, but not users of pyzmq.
